California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
August 14, 1991
Gary H. werner
22861 Ironbark Drive
Dianond Bar, cA 917 65
Re: Your Request For Advice
Our Fi le No. I-91-125
Dear Mr. Werner:
You have requested advj'ce concerning the application of the
conf l ict-of- inteiest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the
rr1s3rr1.1 It appears that your question may also raise issues
under the comnoir law doctrine oi inconpatible offices. 
You shouLd
direct any questions about the doctrine of incompatibLe offices to
your city attorney or the state Attorney General.
OUESTION
Do you have an inherent conflict of interest in concurrently
holding lositions as nayor and city councilmember of Diamond Bar,
field iepresentative for Assenbl)rman Horcher, and contract
planning director of La Habra.
CONCLUS ION
Your holding of concurrent public positions alone wi'I1 not
create a conflict of interest under the Act.
FACTS
In April 1990, you r.rere reelected to the Diamond Bar City
Council. Upon reelection, the City Council. elected you to serve
1 co.r".n.ant Code Sections 81OoO-91015. All statutory references
are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
reguJ,ations appear at 2 california code of ReguJ,ations Sectj-on
18000, et seq. AII references to reguJ-ations are to Title 2,
Division 5 of the california code of ReguLations.
428 J Street, Suite 8oo o P.o. Box 8o7 o Sacramenro cA 958o4-ogo7 . (916) 322.i66o


Our FiIe No. 91- 12 5
Page 2
as mayor. You receive S500 per month from the city of Diamond Bar
for your city council Position.
In December 1990, you accepted an appointment to a field
representative position with Assemblyman PauI v. Horcher.
You are also continuing in your seventeen-year-o Ld city
planning consulting business and are frequentLy retained by local
tities 
as a consultant. As of Decenber 1990, you have been
retained by the city of La Habra Heights in the capacity as
contract consulting 'rplanning director.r' 
You receive monthly
income from La Habra Heights.
DISCUSSION
The Act provides a four-part test to determine $/hether a
pubJ.ic official 
has a conflict of interest in a particular
lovernrnental decision. First, is the official 
naking,
farticipating 
in making, or using his officiat 
position to
influenle a governmentil decision? (Section 8710o.) Second, is
it reasonably foreseeable that the decision wiIl affect the
official's 
eionomic interest? 
(Section 87103-) Third, is the
effect of the decision on the official's 
economic i.nterest
rnaterial? (Id.) 
Fourth, is the effect of the decision on the
official's 
econornic interest distinguishable fron its effect on
the public generally? (Id. ) Each of the questions must be-
answlred in the affirnative in order for there to be a conflict of
interest. 
If there is a conflict of interest, then the public
official 
nust disgualify himself from making or participating in
the decision. Since it aPpears that you are a public official 
in
three offices, the above stated test should be used for each of
your public pos itions .
Although you ask about trinherent" conflicts of interest in
your positions, the Act does not examine rrinherenttt conflicts, but
iather conflict that arises when an official 
makes, participates
in or uses his official position to influence governmental
deci.sion.2 You have not specified any particular decisions.
2 
An official 
makes a governnental decision when he or she
votes, commits his agency to a course of action, enters into a
contract, or appoints soneone. (Regulation 18700(b), copy
enclosed. ) Partj.cipating in the making of a governmental decisons
includes, among other things, advising or naking recornrnendations
to the decision maker. (Regulation 18700(c).) An official
attenpts to use his official position to influence a decision if,
for the purpose of infLuencing the decision, he contacts or
appears before, or otherwise attenpts to influence, any nenber,
officer, 
erflployee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation
1,8700.1(a) . )
A.


Our File No. 9■
―■25
Page 3
However since it is likely that you will be making some decisions
in the future, We prOVide the following advice.
B. Foreseeable Financia] Effect on Economic fnterests
The second issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect the official's 
econonic interests. 
The parameters of a
public official's 
econonic interests are set forth in Section
87103. For the purposes of the question at hand, this includes
(a) Any business entity in which the
public official 
has a direct or indirect
investment worth one thousand dollars (S1,000)
or more.
***
(c) Any source of income, other than
gifts and other than Loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public
without regard t.o official 
status, aggregating
tt^to hundred fifty dollars ($25o) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to
the public official within 12 months prior to
the ti:ne when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the
public official 
is a director, officer.
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of nanagement.
***
Section 871o3.
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; 
however certainty is not required. 
(Downev Cares v.
Downev Conmunitv Developnent Con. (1987) 196 CaI. App. 3d 983,
9a9-99li witt v. llorrow (L977) 7O Cal. App. 3d 817.)
Your letter nentions four possible economic interests:
Assemblyman Horcher's office, the city of Diamond Bar, the city of
La Habra, and your consulting business. Although you may receive
compensation fron the first three, these sources are not
recognized economic interests under the Act. Salary and
reinbursenent for expenses or per dien received frorn a state,
local or federal governnent agency are expressly exempted from the
definition of trinconerr for purposes of the Act. (Section


Our File No. 91-12 5
Page 4
82030(b) (2),' Patland Advice Letter, No. I-89-419.)3 In addition,
because none of your public positions are with an organization or
enterprise operated for profit, they are not a business entities
as defined by the Act. (section 82005.) Thus, your positions
with Assenblyman l{orcher, as nayor and city councilmember for
Dianond Bar, and as planning di.rector for La Habra will not create
a conflict of interest in your decisions in any of the other
posi.tions. (Section 87103 (d) ; Section 82005. )
Your consulting business itself, 
however, is not exempted.
Therefore, when naking or participating in decisions, you should
determine whether the decisions' effect is large enough to be
rnaterial under the law.
c. Materialitv
In order to deterrnine materiality, you must first 
determine
whether your business is directly invo).ved in the decision under
ReguJ.ation La702.1(b) (copy enclosed) . A business is directly
involved in a decision if it, for example, initiates the relevant
governmental proceeding or is the subject of the proceeding.
jRegulation t8'7o2.1(b) .) If your business is directly involved,
rny effect could be deemed material. 
(Regulation la102.1(a) (1).)
If if the effect is not naterial under that test, you should
determine whether the effect is rnaterial under Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed) . Under that regul,ation, you will have to
determine the size of your busi.ness then deternine how much of a
dollar effect the decision will have on your business' Most smal'l
businesses are covered by subdivision (g). 
Under that test, a
decision is naterial if the decision wiII increase or decrease the
business' gross revenues by 91o,ooo or nore, the decision wiII
cause the business to incur or avoid expenses of 52,500 or more,
or the decision wi}l. cause an increase or decrease in assets or
Iiabilities 
by $10,000 or nore. (Regulation !8702.2(S).)
D. PubIic Genera I Iv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financiaL effect of a
decision is naterial, disqual ification is reguired only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect upon the public
generally. 
(Section 87103.) ff the decision does not affect alI
the nembers of the public in the same manner, disqualification 
may
be required unless the effect of the decision is the same as the
effect on a signif j.cant segrnent of the public. 
(Regulation tB7O3,
copy enclosed. ) The trpublic,' is alL the persons in the
jurrisdiction 
of the agency affected by the decision. (In re
Leqal (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, 15.) Thus, the rrpublic', for decisions
3 w" u.==rr,n"
rr consu Itant It
official. 
We
consultant.
that as a contract planning director, you are a
as defined by the Act and therefore a public
have enclosed Regulation 1g7OO, which defines


Our File No. 91-■
25
Page 5
nade as mayor or councilmember of Diamond Bar is the affected
citizenry of Diamond Bar. The "publicrr as a field representative
would be the affected constituents in the Assembly district- 
The
public't as a planning director is the affected citizenry of La
Habra.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-59o]-.r
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel.
4
■n
and
at
り血
ュ
counsel, LegaI Division

Copies of Conrnission Regulations and Opinions are available
many law libraries. 
Alternatively, copies of theses materials
Cornmission advice Letters may be obtained fron the Cornmission
a cost of 10C per page.
